text: Protein crystallization, data collection, and refinement

Crystals of KEO7 were obtained by the microbatch method under oil by using the Oryx6 robot (Douglas Instruments Ltd., East Garston, Hungerford, Berkshire, U.K.). The protein concentration used for crystallization was $2 3 \mathrm { m g / m l }$ and crystals were grown at $4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ from a precipitating solution of $1 0 0 \mathrm { m M }$ Bis Tris propane $\mathrm { p H } 8 . 5 , 0 . 2 \mathrm { M N a F }$ ,and $2 0 \%$ PEG 3500. Crystals formed in space group P6122, with cell constants $\mathrm { a = b } { = } 9 6 . 3 8 \mathring \mathrm { ~ A }$ ， $\mathrm { c } = 1 5 3 . 9 9$ $\mathring \mathrm { A }$ , and contain two monomers in the asymmetric unit cell with $\mathrm { V m }$ of 1.86 A³/Dalton.

Diffraction data to $2 . 2 5 \mathrm { ~ \AA ~ }$ , from a single crystal were collected on a Rigaku R-AXIS $\mathrm { { I V } + + }$ imaging plate area detector using a Rigaku RU-H3R rotating anode operated at $5 \mathrm { k W }$ and Osmic multilayer $\mathbf { X }$ -ray focusing mirrors. Diffraction data were integrated, scaled, and reduced using the HKL2OO program packages1. The structure was solved by molecular replacement using the program PHASERS²,by using the refined structure of the Imidazoleglycerolphosphate synthase from Thermotoga Maritime as a model (PDB code 1THF) as a model.

All steps of atomic refinement were carried out with the program CCP4/Refmac5S3,S4. The models were built to oa-weighted, $2 \mathrm { F _ { o b s } ~ - ~ } \mathrm { F _ { c a l c } }$ ，and $\mathrm { F _ { o b s } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } F _ { c a l c } }$ maps using the program COOTS5. The coordinates of KEO7 have been deposited to the Protein Data Bank with the accession code 2RKX. The KEO7 model was evaluated with the program PROCHECKS6. Details of the data collection and structure refinement are described in Supplementary Table 5 and an overlay of the electron density with relevant residues in the active site is shown in Supplementary Figure 6.

text: For KE07 variants Round 2 11/10D and Round 7 10/11G, $\mathbf { k } _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ and $\mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { M } }$ values were determined with 5-nitrobenzisoxazole at $\mathrm { p H } 4 . 0 – 9 . 0$ Initial velocities $\mathbf { \Pi } ( \mathbf { v } _ { 0 } )$ were determined at eight different substrate concentrations ( $0 . 1 3 \mathrm { - } 1 . 0 5 ~ \mathrm { m M } )$ . For KEO7 variants Round 4 1E/11H, Round 4 1A/7D and Round 7 2/5B, $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } }$ values were determined with 5- nitrobenzisoxazole at $\mathrm { p H } 4 . 0 – 9 . 0$ by measuring the initial velocities $\left( \mathbf { v } _ { 0 } \right)$ with low substrate concentrations ( $\mathbf { \mathrm { 0 . 1 - 0 . 3 ~ m M } }$ . It was impossible to obtain a pH-rate profile for the original KE07 design, because of its low activity.

The buffers used (at $5 0 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ ） were: Citrate (pH 4.0-5.5), MES (pH 5.5-6.5),and Bis-tris propane $\mathrm { ( p H ~ 6 . 5 – 9 . 0 ) }$ at $5 0 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ .The ionic strength was adjusted to a total of O.1M with NaCl. The enzyme stocks were kept in 10 mM HEPES $\mathrm { p H } 7 . 2 5$ containing $1 0 0 \ \mathrm { m M } \ \mathrm { N a C l }$ and $5 \%$ glycerol. 5-nitrobenzisoxazole was used from O.1M stock in acetonitrile,and the co-solvent percentage was equalized to $1 . 5 \ \%$ in all the reaction mixtures. Product formation was monitored spectrophotometrically at $3 8 0 \mathrm { n m }$ (Power HT microtiter scanning spectrophotometer), in $2 0 0 ~ \mu \mathrm { l }$ reaction volumes, using 96-well plates. The reported results are the average of at least three independent measurements.

CD measurements. To control for conformational changes induced by the pH shiftes, far-UV CD wavelengths scans ( $2 6 0 - 2 0 0 \ \mathrm { n m }$ ） were collected on an Aviv 62A DS spectrometer in a $1 \mathrm { m m }$ pathlength cuvette. $4 0 \mathrm { m M }$ protein in 5 mM HEPES $\mathrm { p H } 7 . 2 5 / 1 0 0 \mathrm { m M N a C l }$ was diluted 1:4 with $5 0 ~ \mathrm { m M }$ buffer at varying $\mathrm { p H }$ (see above section for detailed description of buffer used) and incubated at room temperature for $3 0 \ \mathrm { m i n }$ . The protein solution was filtered prior to the measurement and the temperature was maintained at $2 5 \mathrm { ^ \circ C }$ with a Peltier device.

Data analysis. Kinetic parameters $( \mathrm { k _ { c a t } , \ K _ { M } , \ k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } ) }$ were obtained by fitting the data to theMichaelis-Mentenequation $[ \mathrm { v } _ { 0 } { = } k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } [ \mathrm { E } ] _ { 0 } [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } / ( [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } { + } K _ { \mathrm { M } } ) ] .$ ，usingtheprogram Kaleidagraph 5.O. When working with low substrate concentrations, data were fitted to the linear regime of the Michaelis-Menten model $[ \mathrm { v _ { 0 } } ] \mathrm { v _ { 0 } } = \mathrm { \ [ S ] _ { 0 } } [ \mathrm { E ] _ { 0 } } \mathrm { k _ { c a t } } / \mathrm { K _ { M } } ]$ ，and $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } }$ was deduced from the slope. All the data presented are the averages of at least 3 independent experiments with standard deviations. The pH-rate data ( $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } }$ and $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } }$ values for each pH value ( $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } ) ^ { \mathrm { H } }$ and $( k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } / K _ { \mathrm { { M } } } ) ^ { \mathrm { { H } } } )$ were fitted using the equations $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } ) ^ { \mathrm { H } } { = } ( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } ) ^ { \mathrm { m a x } } \mathrm { x } 1 0 ^ { - \mathrm { p K a } } / ( 1 0 ^ { - }$ $^ \mathrm { p H } { + } 1 0 ^ { - \mathrm { p K a l } } $ ）and $\begin{array} { r l } { ( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } ) ^ { \mathrm { H } } { = } ( } & { { } k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } ) ^ { \mathrm { m a x } } \mathrm { x } 1 0 ^ { \mathrm { - p K a } } / ( 1 0 ^ { \mathrm { - p H } } { + } 1 0 ^ { \mathrm { - p K a l } } ) \mathrm { ; } } \end{array}$ where $\left( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } \right) ^ { \mathrm { m a x } }$ and $( k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } } ) ^ { \mathrm { m a x } }$ are the plateau values of $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ ,and $\mathsf { p } K _ { \mathsf { a 1 } }$ is the apparent $\mathrm { p K _ { a } }$ value for the acidic group.

The following plots are of $\mathrm { p H }$ -rate profiles of KEO7 variants. Shown are $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } } ~ \nu s ~ \mathrm { p H }$ and $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } }$ vs $\mathrm { p H }$ for the KEO7 variants Round 2 11/1OD (Supplementary Figure 7 and Supplementary Figure 8, respectively) and Round 7 10/11G (Supplementary Figure 9 and Supplementary Figure 10, respectively); and $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } } / \mathrm { K _ { M } } \nu \mathrm { s } \mathrm { p H }$ for the KEO7 variants Round 4 1E/11H (Supplementary Figure 11)，Round 4 1A/7D (Supplementary Figure 12） and Round 7 2/5B (Supplementary Figure 13). In the legend table,ml denotes the plateau values of $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ and $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ and $\mathrm { m } 2$ denotes the apparent $\mathsf { p K } _ { \mathrm { a } }$ value for the acidic group.

The pH-rate profiles of al the KEO7 variants, with the exception of Round 2 11/1OD, have a fully pronounced acidic shoulder，consistent with the deprotonation of the catalytic Glu101. Activity measurements above pH 9 were precluded both by high background reaction rates and by general protein destabilization (e.g. lysines deprotonation). Below pH 4.5,the activity decrease can be also ascribed to protein destabilization. Indeed, the $\mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { M } }$ values of KEO7 variants increased below $\mathrm { p H } ~ 5$ ，and CD measurements indicated a conformation change at $\mathrm { p H } 4 . 5$ . For the variant Round 2 11/1OD, there was no significant decrease in $\mathbf { k } _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ down to $\mathrm { p H } 5$ , indicating a $\mathrm { p K } _ { \mathrm { a } } \mathrm { < } 4 . 5$ ,and the decrease in $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } }$ below pH 5 is partially attributed to increase in the $\mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { M } }$ .For the variant Round 7 10/11G, $\mathrm { K } _ { \mathrm { M } }$ values did not vary much with $\mathrm { p H }$ ，as indicated by the similarity of the $\mathrm { p H }$ -rate profiles obtained for $\mathbf { k } _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ and $\mathrm { k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } }$

KE07 variants with Ile7Asp mutation (Round 4 1E/11H, Round 7 2/5B and Round 7 10/11G) have $\mathsf { p K } _ { \mathrm { a } }$ values significantly higher than Round 2 variant 11/1OD bearing no mutation or Round 4 1A/7D with Ile7Gln mutation $\mathrm { ( p K _ { a } ( k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } ) }$ of $5 . 4 { - } 5 . 7 \ \nu s \ \mathrm { p K _ { a } ( k _ { c a t } / K _ { M } ) }$ of 4.5-5.O). It can be explained by the fact that Asp7 is breaking the salt bridge between the catalytic Glu101 and Lys222, rendering the catalytic Glu101 more basic.
Validating the Kemp elimination activity of the designed and evolved enzymes
The possibility of a contaminating enzyme being responsible for the observed catalytic activity was ruled out by several experiments.

a. Possible sources of contamination The Kemp elimination is an unnatural reaction, and it is highly probable that no natural enzyme exists, certainly in E. coli, that catalyzes this reaction as its primary，native function. It could may well be that this reaction is catalyzed by certain enzymes in a promiscuous manner, and hence at low efficiency. However, bacterial lysates of the strains used here for over-expression of the designed templates do not exhibit Kemp elimination activity above the buffer blank. Thus, it is highly improbable that the activity observed with the designed templates is coming from endogenous $E$ ，coli enzyme.Further，to account for the observed activities， the contaminating enzyme will have to dominate the preparation (due to its weak catalytic activity） rather than contaminate it at low percentage, and also co-purify with the designed enzymes throughout the process (see Section c below).

b. The wide range of mutant enzyme activities Site directed mutagenesis of the key catalytic residues was performed on both the designed enzymes and the evolved variants (see Table 1, and Supplementary Table 2). In all the cases, mutating the catalytic general base (either Glu, or His) into Gln or Ala caused a significant reduction (in almost all cases,by well over 1Oo-fold,and in some cases by over l0oo-fold） in the catalytic activity,as measured by in both crude cell lysates and the purified proteins. The designed KE07 was further evolved, and a series of variants carrying up to 8 mutations exhibited catalytic activities that are up to 2O0-fold higher than the designed enzymes. It is unlikely that the levels of a contaminating enzyme(s) that co-purified with the designed enzymes will vary to such degree ${ \it \Omega } > 2 0 0 { \it \Omega }$ -fold up,and ${ \scriptstyle \mathrm { > } } 1 0 0 0$ -fold down), let alone through several purification steps.

c. Protein purification Independent protein purifications were performed using different protocols，and in three different laboratories involved in the project,yet the kinetic parameters of the resulting proteins were essentially identical for all preparations.

A.The designed enzymes were purified in the Department of Biochemistry, University of Washington, Seatle, USA - description of proteins purification,co-elution and activity profiles, SDS-PAGE gels, and mass-spectrometry results, are available below. B. The designed KEO7,and its evolved variants,were purified in the Department of Biological Chemistry, Weizmann Institute of Science, Rehovot, Israel. C. The KEO7 template and its evolved variants were also purified at the Israel Structural Proteomics Center, Weizmann Institute of Science. The proteins were purified by Ni-NTA HiTrap chelating HP column (Amersham), followed by gel filtration (HiLoad 16/60 SuperdexTM, Amersham), after which their specific activity and kinetic parameters were measured,and were essentially identical to those measured for these variants in a different preparation (Supplementary Table 6, Table 2). The variants were subjected to crystallization, and the structure of several variants was solved.

text: While the specific activity for 1thf and 1jcl is essentially identical to background, la53, anindole-3-glycerolphosphatesynthase(IGPS)， exhibitsvery low，butabove background,activity. However,the activity of the designed enzymes is far higher than observed with 1a53. More importantly， the introduced catalytic bases of the designed enzymes which use la53 as template scaffold (KE10, KE59,and KE71) are at different sequence positions than the native catalytic residues of IGPS. Furthermore, the active-site mutants show no Kemp elimination activity (Supplementary Figure 24) and all charged residues, which could serve as potential catalytic base, in the indole binding site (which is structurally very similar to the substrate） were re-designed to hydrophobic or aromatic residues for transition state binding. Indeed，when the catalytic base of the designed enzymes was mutated, the activity of most mutants dropped to background (e.g. the E231Q mutant of KE59). These findings strongly suggest that the designed mechanism is new and unrelated to any promiscuous activity and that the enzyme design process did not increase an existing activity.
Determination of $\mathbf { k _ { u n c a t } }$ for hydrolysis of ${ \bf 5 - N O } _ { 2 }$ benzisoxazole
$\mathrm { k _ { u n c a t } }$ was determined as a function of buffer concentration at $\mathrm { p H } ~ 7 . 2 5$ at $2 5 \mathrm { { ^ \circ C } }$ and extrapolated to zero buffer concentration.

In detail, non-enzymatic hydrolysis of $5 { \mathrm { - N O } } _ { 2 }$ benzisoxazole( $\mathbf { \mathrm { 0 . 2 ~ m M } }$ was followed in HEPES $\mathrm { p H } 7 . 2 5$ (10, 25, 50,75,100 mM), by monitoring the absorbance at $3 8 0 \mathrm { n m }$ for 5 days. The plots were fit to the pseudo first order equation: $\mathrm { O D } = \bf { C } _ { \alpha } ( 1 - e ^ { - k t } )$ , where OD is the absorbance at $3 8 0 \mathrm { n m }$ , C is a constant, $\mathbf { k }$ is the $\mathrm { k _ { u n c a t } }$ ,and t is time in minutes.

The $\mathrm { k _ { u n c a t } }$ value for zero buffer concentration was obtained by ploting the $\mathrm { k _ { u n c a t } }$ values obtained for various HEPES concentrations and extrapolating to $0 ~ \mathrm { \ m M }$ HEPES (Supplementary Figure 25).
